true_relatives.note: Hindi बिस्तर is borrowed directly from Persian bistar — the word and its meaning are identical, reflecting the Persian cultural influence on Hindi through the Mughal period

useful_coincidences.note: No shared origin, but the similar sound can help: a bistro is where you sit and rest — just like a बिस्तर is where you lie down and rest

sound_alikes.note: 'bistro' sounds like the first two syllables of बिस्तर — imagine a tiny bistro where the chairs convert into beds at night

evolution.meaning: bed, bedding spread on the floor

root_breakdown.logic: The Persian bistar described bedding spread out on the floor — the traditional way of sleeping across much of the Persian-speaking world. Hindi adopted both the word and the concept.

etymology_story: The word comes from Persian bistar, meaning bedding or a sleeping place spread out on the ground.
Through centuries of Persian-language Mughal rule, dozens of domestic Persian words entered everyday Hindi.
Today बिस्तर means any bed — from a floor mat to a modern frame with mattress — and is indispensable in daily Hindi speech.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the first syllable; the second syllable is short

tips.tip: the first two syllables of बिस्तर sound like 'bistro' — bis-tar versus bis-tro

memory_hook.primary: A rolled-up bedding bundle tied with rope, leaning against a wall — the classic Indian बिस्तरबंद ready for travel
memory_hook.connection: The rolled bedding is the most literal image of बिस्तर — the Persian original meant bedding spread out, and this image shows it before and after spreading

examples.english: Lie down on the bed.
The bed hasn't been made yet.
Her bed is very comfortable.

examples.hindi: तकिया और कम्बल बिस्तर पर रखे हैं।
examples.romanisation: takiyā aur kambal bistar par rakhe haĩ.
examples.english: The pillow and blanket are on the bed.
examples.literal: pillow and blanket bed on placed are

true_relatives.meaning: a support to lean on; a Sufi hospice
true_relatives.note: Hindi तकिया comes from the Arabic takkiya — a place or object to rest against. The Arabic word also named Sufi lodges where travellers rested, linking rest and support across both meanings

sound_alikes.note: 'tacky' starts with the same 'tak' sound — imagine a tacky (sticky) pillow that won't let go of your head

root.meaning: to lean, to recline, to support oneself

evolution.meaning: to lean; a support, a resting place

root_breakdown.logic: The Arabic root meant to lean or rest against something; the derived noun takkiya named anything you lean on — a support, a cushion, and eventually the household pillow.

etymology_story: The Arabic root taka'a meant to lean or recline against a support, and the noun takkiya described any resting place or object.
In classical Arabic culture, a takkiya also named a Sufi hospice — a place of rest for travellers and seekers.
Through Persian and Urdu, the word narrowed to the domestic pillow and entered Hindi as तकिया.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the first syllable; yā at the end is long

tips.tip: the 'ta-ki' part sounds like the start of 'taqueria' — same stress and vowel in the first two syllables

memory_hook.primary: A plump white pillow with Arabic calligraphy embroidered across its cover, resting on a bed
memory_hook.connection: The Arabic calligraphy on the pillow signals the Arabic origin of तकिया, and the pillow itself is the meaning — the object you lean and rest on

examples.english: I want a soft pillow.

examples.hindi: तकिया बिस्तर के ऊपर रखा है।
examples.romanisation: takiyā bistar ke ūpar rakhā hai.
examples.english: The pillow is on the bed.
examples.literal: pillow bed of above placed is

examples.english: Bring two pillows.

examples.hindi: तकिये के साथ चादर भी बदलो।
examples.romanisation: takiye ke sāth cādar bhī badlo.
examples.english: Change the bed sheet along with the pillow cover.

true_relatives.meaning: sheet, large cloth; also the full-body covering worn by women
true_relatives.note: Hindi चादर is borrowed from Persian chādar — both refer to a large sheet of cloth. In Persian and Urdu, the same word also names the full-body covering garment worn over clothing

sound_alikes.note: चादर sounds somewhat like 'charter' — imagine chartering (booking) fresh bed sheets for a hotel room

evolution.meaning: bed sheet; also a shroud or wrap

root_breakdown.logic: The Persian chādar named any large expanse of cloth — a bed sheet, a body wrap, or a covering. Hindi adopted the word with the same broad meaning, though in everyday use चादर most commonly means a bed sheet.

etymology_story: Persian chādar simply meant a large piece of cloth — the kind used to cover a bed, wrap around a body, or drape a surface.
Through Mughal cultural influence, the word entered Urdu and from there into everyday Hindi speech.
Today चादर is the standard word for a bed sheet, though the word still carries echoes of its broader meaning — any large cloth covering.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the first long syllable

tips.tip: the first syllable 'cā' sounds like 'char' — long 'aa' vowel, just like in 'Char' or 'taar'

memory_hook.primary: A crisp white cotton sheet billowing in the wind as someone shakes it out over a bed in a sunlit room
memory_hook.connection: The billowing sheet — large, white, spread across the entire bed — is exactly what चादर means; the image captures its size and domestic role

examples.english: Change the bed sheet.

examples.hindi: यह चादर बहुत पुरानी हो गई है।
examples.romanisation: yah cādar bahut purānī ho gaī hai.
examples.english: This bed sheet has become very old.
examples.literal: this sheet very old become gone is

examples.english: Spread a clean sheet on the bed.

examples.hindi: ठंड में एक चादर से काम नहीं चलता।
examples.romanisation: ṭhaṃḍ mẽ ek cādar se kām nahī̃ caltā.
examples.english: In the cold, one sheet isn't enough.
examples.literal: cold in one sheet from work not moves

true_relatives.note: Hindi कम्बल is unchanged from Sanskrit kambala — one of those words that has barely changed over two thousand years and still means exactly the same thing

useful_coincidences.note: No shared origin, but both start with 'cam/kam' — a blanket covers and hides, just as camouflage does

sound_alikes.note: 'gamble' rhymes with 'kambal' — imagine gambling on whether you'll be warm enough with only one blanket on a cold night

root_breakdown.logic: Sanskrit kambala was specifically a woollen blanket — the warmth-giving cloth wrap. The word passed into Hindi virtually unchanged, remaining one of the most stable vocabulary items across millennia.

etymology_story: Sanskrit kambala named a woollen blanket — the thick cloth wrap used against cold.
The word is remarkable for its stability: it has passed through two thousand years of linguistic change without losing either its form or its meaning.
Today कम्बल is used for any blanket — woollen, synthetic, or cotton — and is one of the most recognisable words in Hindi.

tips.tip: कम्बल rhymes with 'gamble' — the 'ambal' ending is the same

memory_hook.primary: A thick dark woollen blanket wrapped around a shivering person sitting by a fireplace, with the Sanskrit letters visible in the weave of the fabric
memory_hook.connection: The woollen warmth-wrap is exactly what Sanskrit kambala described — the image is a direct translation of the word's two-thousand-year-old meaning

examples.english: Wrap yourself in the blanket at night.
This blanket is very warm.

examples.hindi: बिस्तर पर तकिया और कम्बल दोनों रखे हैं।
examples.romanisation: bistar par takiyā aur kambal donõ rakhe haĩ.
examples.english: Both the pillow and blanket are on the bed.
examples.literal: bed on pillow and blanket both placed are

examples.english: In winter you need a thick blanket.

true_relatives.note: Hindi अलमारी comes directly from Portuguese armário — brought to India by Portuguese traders and colonists in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries

useful_coincidences.note: The Arabic definite article 'al-' appears at the start of अलमारी, giving it an Arabic-flavoured sound even though its true origin is Portuguese via Latin

sound_alikes.note: 'armoire' (the English word for a large wardrobe, borrowed from French) sounds very similar to अलमारी — both descend from the same Latin root and mean the same furniture piece

root.meaning: a chest or cabinet for storing arms and tools

root_breakdown.logic: A Latin arms-storage cabinet became the Portuguese word for any household storage cupboard, and then entered Hindi through Portuguese colonial contact, where it became the standard word for wardrobe.

etymology_story: The Latin armarium was a cabinet for storing weapons or tools — the word is related to 'arms' and 'armour'.
In Portuguese, armário became the general word for any large household storage cupboard or wardrobe.
Portuguese colonists brought the word to India, and it entered Hindi as अलमारी — now the everyday word for any freestanding cupboard or wardrobe.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the long second syllable mā

tips.tip: अलमारी sounds very close to the English 'armoire' — both have the 'ar/al' beginning and a long middle syllable

memory_hook.primary: A tall wooden wardrobe standing in a bedroom, its doors open to reveal neatly folded saris and shirts inside, with a small Portuguese sailing ship carved on its door panel
memory_hook.connection: The carved ship signals that अलमारी arrived in India by sea from Portugal — the furniture piece and its name both came with the Portuguese

examples.english: Put the clothes in the wardrobe.
Where is the key to the wardrobe?

examples.hindi: कम्बल अलमारी के ऊपर रखा है।
examples.romanisation: kambal almārī ke ūpar rakhā hai.
examples.english: The blanket is on top of the wardrobe.
examples.literal: blanket wardrobe of above placed is

examples.english: We need a big wardrobe.
